coredata.dc:title: A dual-mode colorimetric and fluorometric nanosensor for detection of uric acid based on N, P co-doped carbon dots and in-situ formation of Au/Ag core-shell nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Hallaj, Tooba
Azizi, Neda
Amjadi, Mohammad
coredata.dc:description: Green emissive N,P co-doped carbon dots (N,P-CDs) were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method from citric acid, urea, and phosphoric acid as carbon, nitrogen and phosphorous pre-sources, respectively. The prepared N,P-CDs were applied to establish a dual-mode colorimetric and fluorometric sensor for monitoring - uric acid based on in-situ formation of Ag shell on the surface of AuNPs. We observed that the fluorescence intensity of N,P-CDs was quenched by adding AuNPs due to overlap between the emission peak of N,P-CDs and surface plasmon resonance peak (SPR) of AuNPs. With adding Ag+ and uric acid, the SPR peak intensity of AuNPs increased due to the reduction of Ag+ to Ag0 and Ag shell formation on the surface of AuNPs. Also, a blue shift in the SPR peak position of AuNPs was observed at the higher concentration of uric acid, resulting in the color variation of the solution from red to orange. These variations in the SPR peak intensity and position caused more fluorescence quenching of N,P-CDs because of the increasing spectral overlap between N,P-CDs and Au@AgNPs. We indicated that there is a logarithmic relationship between increasing SPR peak intensity of AuNPs as well as fluorescence quenching of N,P-CDs and uric acid concentration in the range of 0.1–10 and 0.5–10 μM, respectively. Regarding the mentioned facts, we developed a dual colorimetric and fluorometric sensor to determine uric acid. The detection limit was 40 and 400 μM for colorimetric and fluorometric methods, respectively. The established sensor was exploited for uric acid analysis in human urine samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-mode sensor
Au/Ag core-shell nanoparticles
Doped carbon dots
Inner filter effect
Uric acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of uric acid in urine samples
Mechanism of colorimetric and fluorometric assays
Analytical performance for uric acid detection
Uric acid analysis in real sample

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thank Urmia University of Medical Sciences for the support provided (Project number: 2623, approved by the Research Ethic Committee at the Urmia University of Medical Sciences, Ethical code: IR.UMSU.REC.1396.355).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of N, P-CDs, (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of N, P-CDs excited at 360–440 nm wavelength range, with increments of 20 nm, and (c) absorption spectra of N, P-CDs.
(a) FT-IR and (b) EDS spectra of N,P-CDs.

(A) Absorption spectra of Au NPs and (B) fluorescence spectra of N,P-CDs in the absence and presence of Ag+ and uric acid; Conditions: buffer (3.75 × 10−2 M, pH = 11.5), Ag+ (4 mM), Au NPs volume (500 µL), temperature (50 °C), time (10 min) and uric acid 1 µM.
TEM image of a) Au@Ag NPs b) AuNPs.

a) Absorption and b) fluorescence spectra of Au NPs in the presence of Ag+ and different concentrations of uric acid; Inset (a) and (b) are calibration curves for the determination of uric acid. Conditions: buffer (3.75 × 10−2 M, pH = 11.5), Ag+ (4 mM), Au NPs volume (500 µL), temperature (50 °C), time (10 min), N, P-CDs, 100 µL.
Comparison of the established sensor with some previously reported methods for the determination of uric acid.
Tolerance limits of some potentially interfering species in the determination of 0.5 and 1 µM uric acid by developed colorimetric and fluorometric methods, respectively.

row.entry: Na+, K+, F−, NO3 −, SO4 2−, Zn2+, Sucrose, Glucose, Lactose, Alanine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Result for the determination of uric acid in urine samples.

head.ce:title: A dual-mode colorimetric and fluorometric nanosensor for detection of uric acid based on N, P co-doped carbon dots and in-situ formation of Au/Ag core-shell nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Cellular and Molecular Research Center, Cellular and Molecular Medicine Institute, Urmia University of Medical Sciences, Urmia 5714783734, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Cellular and Molecular Research Center, Cellular and Molecular Medicine Institute, Urmia University of Medical Sciences, Urmia 5714783734, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: N,P-CDs and AuNPs/Ag+ were exploited to design a dual-mode colorimetric and fluorometric sensor for the determination of uric acid based on in-situ formation Au@Ag core shell NPs and inner filter effect.

Green emissive N,P co-doped carbon dots (N,P-CDs) were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method from citric acid, urea, and phosphoric acid as carbon, nitrogen and phosphorous pre-sources, respectively. The prepared N,P-CDs were applied to establish a dual-mode colorimetric and fluorometric sensor for monitoring - uric acid based on in-situ formation of Ag shell on the surface of AuNPs. We observed that the fluorescence intensity of N,P-CDs was quenched by adding AuNPs due to overlap between the emission peak of N,P-CDs and surface plasmon resonance peak (SPR) of AuNPs. With adding Ag+ and uric acid, the SPR peak intensity of AuNPs increased due to the reduction of Ag+ to Ag0 and Ag shell formation on the surface of AuNPs. Also, a blue shift in the SPR peak position of AuNPs was observed at the higher concentration of uric acid, resulting in the color variation of the solution from red to orange. These variations in the SPR peak intensity and position caused more fluorescence quenching of N,P-CDs because of the increasing spectral overlap between N,P-CDs and Au@AgNPs. We indicated that there is a logarithmic relationship between increasing SPR peak intensity of AuNPs as well as fluorescence quenching of N,P-CDs and uric acid concentration in the range of 0.1–10 and 0.5–10 μM, respectively. Regarding the mentioned facts, we developed a dual colorimetric and fluorometric sensor to determine uric acid. The detection limit was 40 and 400 μM for colorimetric and fluorometric methods, respectively. The established sensor was exploited for uric acid analysis in human urine samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Uric acid, one of the primary antioxidants in human biological fluids, is the final product of catabolism of purine nucleosides. The amount of uric acid in the human body can be affected by lifestyle and diet. Inbred disorders of purine metabolism can cause hyperuricemia and hypouricemia in the human body, causing health problems such as hypertension, cardiovascular disease, metabolic syndrome, kidney failure or stones, and gout [1]. Therefore, the biological monitoring of uric acid is of great importance, and diverse analytical methods including chromatography [2,3], electrochemistry [4,5], and spectroscopy methods [6–9] have been employed for this purpose. Among them, colorimetric and fluorometric methods gain attention due to their promising properties, and some fluorometric metods based on AuNPs was designed to determine uric acid in biological fluid [10,11]. The simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and visual detection possibility make colorimetric methods the desired choice for biological analysis. On the other hand, fluorometric probes have been widely applied to detect various biomolecules due to their sensitivity and selectivity. The desirable properties of both methods can be achieved by combining them and developing dual colorimetric and fluorometric sensors. It is worth noting that more reliable results would be obtained by a probe with dual signals. Based on these facts, recently nanomaterial-based dual sensors have been reported for the assay of different analytes, including uric acid.

In almost all of these sensors, noble metal nanoparticles with divers shape and combination [12–14], as well as fluorophores, including organic days [15], metal nanoclusters [11], Si NPs [16], and carbon-based quantum dots [12], have acted as colorimetric and fluorometric indicators, respectively. The well-known property of noble metal nanoparticles, which is extensively exploited to establish various colorimetric sensors, is localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR). Divers parameters including the shape of nanoparticles and environmental conditions determine the position and intensity of LSPR peak [17,18]. For example, with deposition of Ag0 on the surface of AuNPs, resulting in the formation of Au@Ag core–shell nanoparticles (Au@AgNPs), not only a blue shift in the LSPR peak position of AuNPs can be observed but also the intensity of this peak would be improved. Concerning this phenomenon, growth-based colorimetric sensors can be designed for the determination of different compounds including biomolecules [19–22]. This kind of probe is more appropriate for biological analysis than aggregation-based colorimetric probes which suffer from disadvantages such as low selectivity [23,24].

On the other hand, due to the outstanding features of carbon-based quantum dots (CDs), they have attracted tremendous attention in various fields. The main property of CDs is their multicolor photoluminescence (PL) which makes them a suitable candidate for designing fluorometric sensors [25,26]. Wide varieties of CDs with different emission colors have been reported. The maximum emission wavelength of CDs is tunable depended on several factors including the synthesis pre-sources and condition [27]. For example doping CDs with heteroatoms such as phosphor (P) and nitrogen (N), which act as an n-type donor, can create new defects in the structure of CDs resulting in the improvement and alteration of the optical properties of CDs such as emission wavelength and quantum yield [28,29]. Nowadays doped CDs with noble metal nanoparticles are utilized to develop dual colorimetric and fluorometric sensors [12,13,30–32]. It is worth remarking that adjustment of the maximum emission wavelength of CDs is possible by controlling their preparation condition for accomplishing the highest spectral overlap between CDs and noble metal nanoparticles. Herein, we prepared a green emissive N,P doped CDs in formamide solution by a simple hydrothermal method. Then as-prepared N,P-CDs were applied to design a dual colorimetric and fluorometric sensor for the determination of uric acid based on in-situ formation Au@Ag core–shell NPs and inner filter effect. The developed sensor was exploited to determine uric acid in the humane biological fluids.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM; Leo 906, Zeiss, Germany) was used for TEM imaging. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR; Tensor 27, Bruker) and spectrophotometer (SPEKOL 2000, Analytik Jena, Germany) were employed to measure FT-IR and UV/vis spectra, respectively. The fluorescence spectra were obtained by F-2500 spectrofluorimeter (Hitachi, Japan).

Citric acid, formamide, phosphoric acid, glycine, sodium citrate, silver nitrate, and uric acid were ordered from Merck. HAuCl4·3H2O was purchased from Sigma. Deionized water was used throughout the experiments.

N,P-CDs were synthesized using citric acid (0.21 g) and urea (0.18 g) dissolved in 2.5 mL phosphoric acid and 2.5 mL formamide by a hydrothermal method. After transferring the mentioned mixture into a Teflon equipped stainless steel autoclave, it was heated at 160 °C for 4 h. Then, the autoclave was naturally cool down to ambient temperature. The obtained solution was centrifuged for removing larger particles and then was purified by dialyzing against distilled water for 24 h (using the dialysis bag, 2000 MWCO). The final volume of dialyzed N,P-CDs was adjusted in 20 mL for further experiments.

AuNPs were prepared by common citrate reduction method using HAuCl4 and trisodium citrate. 100 mL of HAuCl4 solution (0.25 mM) was taken to a vigorous boil with stirring, and then 5 mL of trisodium citrate (1%) was added to the solution. The obtained solution was heated under stirring until the solution color was changed to a deep red which indicated the formation of AuNPs. After cooling down to room temperature, the volume of prepared AuNPs was reached 100 mL and stored at 4 °C.

500 µL of AuNPs, 75 µL of buffer (glycine, 1.0 M, pH = 11.5), an appropriate volume of uric acid standard or sample solution, and 400 µL of AgNO3 (0.02 M) were added to a test tube and the obtained mixture was diluted to 2 mL with deionized water. After 10 min incubation at 50 °C, the absorption spectrum of each solution was measured in a 1.0 cm quartz cuvette. The fluorescence measurements were performed after adding 100 µL of N,P-CDs to the solution mentioned above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of uric acid in urine samples
ce:section.ce:para: A 500-µL of human urine samples was put in a centrifuge tube and pretreated by adding 2 mL of Ba(OH)2 0.1 M and 1.8 mL of ZnSO4 0.1 M. The mixture was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min. Afterward, the clear supernatant solution was diluted to 5 mL into a volumetric flask by deionized water. The suitable volume of this solution was assayed according to the procedure mentioned above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of colorimetric and fluorometric assays
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of N,P-CDs and AuNPs was examined to establish a dual-mode colorimetric and fluorometric sensor for monitoring uric acid based on in-situ formation Ag shell on the surface of AuNPs and inner filter effect. Concerning the reducing property of uric acid (with E0 ≤ 0.5 V, at the alkaline condition), we investigated its ability to reduce Ag+ (with E0 = 0.799 V) on the surface of AuNPs (as seeds) to form Ag shell. As shown in Fig. 3 A, with uric acid addition into the experimental solution, containing AuNPs and Ag+, the SPR peak intensity of AuNPs increased. Considering that the extinction coefficient of AgNPs is larger than that of AuNPs, this increase can be attributed to the conversion of Ag+ to Ag0 and the formation of Ag shell on the surface of AuNPs. Moreover, a slight blue shift in SPR peak position of AuNPs was observed at the higher concentration of uric acid, resulting in color variation of solution from red to orange, which can be assigned to the formation of Au@AgNPs with SPR peak at lower wavelength [22,35]. For further confirming of our assertion, the TEM images of AuNPs were studied in the absence and presence of uric acid and Ag+ which indicated the deposition of Ag0 on the AuNPs and formation of Au@AgNPs with size distribution ~ 40 nm (Fig. 4 a). Additionally, the existence of Ag atoms in these NPs was confirmed by EDS analysis (Fig. S1). On the other hand, the fluorescence intensity of N,P-CDs was quenched by adding AuNPs due to overlap between the emission band of N,P-CDs(with λem = 515 nm) and SPR spectrum of AuNPs, (with λmax = 520 nm) (Fig. S2), resulting in inner filter effect [32,36]. We observed that by adding uric acid and Ag+, the extent of fluorescence quenching by AuNPs increased (Fig. 3B). It can be attributed to the more significant spectral overlap between N,P-CDs and formed Au@AgNPs compared to AuNPs. The observed increase in the SPR peak of AuNPs and decrease in florescence intensity of N,P-CDs in the presence of uric acid can be employed as an analytical signal for its detection.

As shown in Fig. 3, some control experiments indicated that without uric acid or Ag+, no variation is observed in the fluorescence and absorption spectra of N,P-CDs and AuNPs. Furthermore, as can be seen in the obtained fluorescence and SPR spectra of uric acid/N,P-CDs and uric acid/AuNPs, there is no interaction between uric acid and N,P-CDs, or AuNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance for uric acid detection
ce:section.ce:para: We also evaluated the selectivity of established methods for uric acid analysis toward some species found in biological samples. The obtained results are reported in Table 2 , indicating the tolerable concentration ratios of interfering species (based on relative error of < 5%). Although some interference in the uric acid assay was obtained for ascorbic acid, the common concentration of uric acid in biological fluids is much higher than that of ascorbic acid. Therefore, it would not interfere with the uric acid assay in biological samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Uric acid analysis in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: To indicate the applicability of the developed dual-mode sensor for monitoring uric acid in the real biological sample, both methods were exploited to measure uric acid in humane urine samples. The obtained data are summarized in Table 3 . Furthermore, for validation of developed methods, we compared the obtained results of uric acid measurement by both methods with those obtained by the standard spectrophotometric method, which confirmed the accuracy of developed methods for real sample analysis.

In summary, a dual-mode colorimetric and fluorometric sensor for the determination of uric acid was designed by N,P-CDs and AuNPs/Ag+. N,P-CDs were prepared from citric acid and urea in the mixture of phosphoric acid and formamide to achieve green emissive dots which indicated desirable spectral overlap with AuNPs. We indicated that by adding uric acid as a reducing agent, Ag+ can be reduced to Ag0 on the surface of AuNPs, resulting in Au@AgNPs with the more intense SPR peak. This assertion was confirmed with TEM imaging and EDS analysis which verified the formation of Au@AgNPs. Owing to an increase in the absorbance of Au@AgNPs, the spectral overlap between emission of N,P-CDs and absorption of Au@AgNPs also increased which caused more fluorescence quenching compared to AuNPs. We indicated that the observed increase of SPR peak intensity of AuNPs and the amount of fluorescence diminishing of N,P-CDs were proportional with uric acid concentration. Therefore, we established a dual colorimetric and fluorometric sensor to determine uric acid in human urine samples with acceptable results.

Tooba Hallaj: Conceptualization, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Neda Azizi: Investigation, Validation. Mohammad Amjadi: Resources, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank Urmia University of Medical Sciences for the support provided (Project number: 2623, approved by the Research Ethic Committee at the Urmia University of Medical Sciences, Ethical code: IR.UMSU.REC.1396.355).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105865.

ce:section.ce:para:
